title: Blood on Cotton
stainChemistry: Blood proteins (hemoglobin, fibrin, albumin) bond to cotton cellulose through hydrogen bonding. Cotton is robust and can tolerate heat, enzymes, and oxidizing bleach. Unlike protein stains on delicate fibers, blood on white cotton can be treated aggressively once initial protein is flushed. The challenge shifts to hemoglobin's iron oxidation state—fresh stains are red/pink, but as hemoglobin oxidizes to ferric state, the stain browns and becomes harder to remove.
whyThisWorks: Cold water first halts protein setting and flushes initial stain. Protein enzymes then digest remaining protein fragments. H₂O₂ oxidizes residual hemoglobin pigment to colorless products. White cotton's chemical tolerance (up to 95°C, oxygen bleach, enzymes, even chlorine) makes this the fastest and most reliable removal sequence.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White cotton terry towel, spray bottle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work from the back of the garment first. Spray cold water and blot hard from underneath—this hydraulically flushes hemoglobin out before it sets. Flip to front and continue flooding and blotting. Blood should visibly lighten in the water. Keep working until blotting produces clear or pale pink water. Speed matters here—protein sets fastest in the first 15-30 minutes.

spottingProtocol.agent: Enzyme spotter (e.g. Biz, Zout, or enzyme pre-soak)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply an enzyme-based pre-soak or spotter (Biz, Zout, or any biological detergent with protease enzymes) directly to the stained area. Work into the fabric gently with a soft brush — enzymes break the peptide bonds in hemoglobin, reducing the protein to smaller fragments that rinse away. Allow to dwell 10-15 minutes at room temperature. Cold water only at this stage — heat permanently sets protein. Do not rub aggressively; the enzymes are doing the molecular work.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dropper or spray bottle, white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pour 3% H₂O₂ (drugstore strength) or 6% if you can source it, directly onto any remaining pink/red stain. Watch the peroxide foam and bubble—this is the oxidation reaction. The stain will fade visibly as hemoglobin pigment is oxidized to colorless compounds. For white cotton, this is highly effective. The peroxide will also gently brighten the fibers around the stain. After 3-5 minutes, blot thoroughly. The smell will be sharp and chemical.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.

Rinse thoroughly under running cold water until all peroxide and enzyme residue is removed. Then wash in warm water with regular detergent. Incomplete rinsing leaves chemistry on the fabric that causes problems later.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates the solution.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use hot water on blood — heat sets protein stains.

homeSolutions.instruction: Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface. Scrape off any dried blood residue with a spoon, working gently from center outward.
Dab the stain lightly with cool water applied to a white cloth, lifting blood from the fibers.
Apply detergent/ammonia solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 1/2 tsp ammonia + 4 oz cool water) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Ammonia can be strong — ensure good ventilation.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working from center outward to remove excess solution.
Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% mixed with ammonia (equal parts) and allow to sit for 3 minutes. This oxidizes remaining blood residue.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on hidden area first — peroxide may lighten some dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply white vinegar solution to neutralize and remove any rings. Rinse again with cool water. Wipe rings outward from center with white cloth.
If stain persists or if fabric is delicate (silk, wool, fine cotton), call a professional cleaner.

materialWarnings: DO NOT use bleach on colored cotton—will fade dye
AVOID extended soaking in H₂O₂ on colored trim or thread
TEST any bleach on a hidden area first—even white cotton can have colored stitching
escalation: If stain persists after H₂O₂ and OxiClean, the hemoglobin has likely oxidized to an iron oxide that requires professional reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) or specialized enzymatic systems. Professional cleaners also have access to higher-strength oxidizers safe for cotton.

household.name: 3% H₂O₂
household.note: Oxidizes hemoglobin to colorless compounds. Safe for white cotton. Apply direct to stain.

household.name: OxiClean
household.note: Sodium percarbonate—oxygen bleach. Safer than chlorine for colored items. Soak 30-60 minutes.

household.name: Clorox Bleach
household.note: For white cotton ONLY. Dilute 1:10 with water. Very effective on fresh blood. 5-10 minutes soak.

professional.name: Protein (concentrated)
professional.note: Professional-strength enzyme for blood protein. More active than household versions.

professional.name: StreePene (Sodium Hydrosulfite)
professional.note: Reduces oxidized hemoglobin (brown stains) back to soluble state. For set-in blood.

customerExplanation: Blood proteins bond to cotton fibers. Cold water flushes initial protein before it sets. Enzymes break down remaining protein, and H₂O₂ oxidizes hemoglobin pigment to colorless compounds. White cotton is very forgiving—we can use aggressive chemistry that wouldn't be safe on delicate fabrics. If heat has been applied before treatment, permanent staining may occur and removal becomes difficult.
commonMistakes: Using hot water first—this speeds protein setting. Always start cold.
Skipping H₂O₂—enzymes remove protein, but peroxide removes the pigment. Together they're powerful; apart, neither fully works.
Not rinsing completely—leftover peroxide and bleach residue will cause yellowing or continued fading days later.
scienceNote: Hemoglobin's iron center undergoes two oxidation state changes. Fresh blood is ferrous (Fe²⁺) and red/pink. Within hours, it oxidizes to ferric (Fe³⁺) and browns. The problem: ferric iron is harder to remove because it's already in a stable, bonded state. H₂O₂ targets fresh (ferrous) hemoglobin directly. Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) converts ferric back to ferrous, making it susceptible to oxidation again. This is why fresh blood comes out with peroxide, but old blood needs reducing bleach.
callPro: If stain is days old and brown/black (oxidized hemoglobin), or if H₂O₂ doesn't fully remove pink color after 5 minutes, call a professional. They'll use reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) to reverse the iron oxidation state, then oxidizing systems to lift color.
solventNote: Professional shops: Fresh blood on white cotton responds well to Protein followed by H₂O₂ or sodium percarbonate. For oxidized stains (brown), StreePene (sodium hydrosulfite 0.5-1% solution) in warm water (40°C) for 10-15 minutes, then thorough rinse and wash. Chlorine bleach is fastest but oxidizing systems (H₂O₂, OxiClean) are safer for color preservation on trim.
aftercare: White cotton tolerates heat well. Machine dryer on medium or high heat is fine. Line dry is also fine. Hang in sunlight if possible—UV light has mild H₂O₂ for white/light fabrics only. Heat will not re-set blood stains on cotton as it does on delicate fibers.
